text:
As of April 15, 2026

The AI Photonics sector lit up today, led by $SIVEF and $SIVE ripping 35% and 30%. Sivers Semiconductors announced a collaboration with Jabil to develop a 1.6T LRO transceiver module using Sivers' DFB InP lasers for hyperscale AI datacenters at 2.5x lower power draw. (https://t.co/j4drYtj4on)

$ALMU's +16% layered on top, digesting its $4M+ U.S. government contract win from Monday. The Jabil deal confirms DFB laser sourcing as a critical bottleneck in the 800G-to-1.6T migration. Transceiver module names lagging on tariff overhang, not demand breakdown.

$SIVEF: +35.23% 
$SIVE: +30.20% 
$ALMU: +16.62% 
$IQE: +12.97% 
$SOI: +6.19% 
$SLOIF: +4.57% 
$POET: +3.87% 
$CIEN: +1.83%
$NDX: +1.40% 
$FORM: +1.04% 
$SPX: +0.80% 
$TSEM: +0.71% 
$MRVL: +0.58% 
$TER: -0.15% 
$GFS: -0.21% 
$ATEYY: -0.47% 
$LWLG: -1.06% 
$AEHR: -1.56% 
$KEYS: -1.58% 
$COHR: -1.67% 
$BESIY: -1.81% 
$AAOI: -2.62% 
$GLW: -2.63% 
$LITE: -3.37% 
$VIAV: -3.73% 
$AXTI: -6.49%

1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Photonic Laser Sources

SIVEF, SIVE, IQE, SOI, SLOIF: Today's clear winner bucket. Jabil deal is a direct read-through for InP photonics suppliers, with the market pricing in compound semiconductor wafer tightness as 1.6T module demand scales into 2H26.

AXTI, TSEM, GFS: AXTI down 6.5% on its own supply-demand timing mismatch, not thesis deterioration. TSEM and GFS caught a modest SiPho/specialty foundry bid but lack the direct InP laser catalyst.

2. Photonic Integrated Circuits & Early-Stage Materials

ALMU: Carrying Monday's $4M+ government contract momentum into today. Quantum dot laser and nonlinear photonic materials funding is direct AI datacom and quantum compute positioning. Watch the AngelTech panel April 20.

POET, LWLG, BESIY: Risk-on sympathy trade. Today's catalyst is InP-specific, so EO polymer and other platform stories get lower beta. No fundamental change.

3. Optical Transceivers & Active Networking

CIEN: Steadiest name in the group, lower tariff exposure than pure module assemblers. AAOI, LITE, COHR, GLW, VIAV all red on supply chain repricing under current trade policy. Demand thesis intact.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test & Infrastructure

MRVL: Holding well. PAM4 DSP silicon wins regardless of which laser or module vendor takes the socket. AEHR, FORM, KEYS, TER soft across the board; no direct read-through from a laser collaboration print. KEYS' new 220 GHz lightwave analyzer is strategically relevant but not a near-term catalyst today.

of April 14, 2026 (removed some players from the list)

The AI photonics and optical infrastructure sector is showing a sharp split today, with substrate and materials plays surging while early-stage and speculative names get hammered. $SOI led the watchlist with an +11.91% move, extending a parabolic run fueled by accelerating investor conviction in Soitec's Photonics-SOI substrate business for co-packaged optics and AI datacenter interconnects. Morningstar noted that Soitec's shares rose 50% in just five trading sessions as investors priced in a bullish outlook for the firm's silicon photonics segment. Morningstar (https://t.co/djE1rX2T9F)
The broader sector is being reshaped by a clear bifurcation: capital is rotating into names with tangible substrate and test infrastructure exposure to the AI optical buildout, while pre-revenue photonic integration plays are getting punished. $POET dropped after Wolfpack Research disclosed a short position, calling the company "an obvious stock promotion" and flagging potential PFIC tax liabilities for U.S. shareholders ahead of the April 15 filing deadline. https://t.co/RRfdqNGIy4 $SIVEF fell in sympathy as the short report reinforced broader skepticism toward pre-revenue photonic chip names. Meanwhile, SPX +2.21% and NDX +1.66% confirm this is a risk-on tape where the photonics laggards are underperforming on idiosyncratic, not macro, weakness.

$POET: -8.01%
$SIVEF: -11.50%
1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
$SOI, $BESIY, $TSEM, $AXTI: The substrate tier is where capital is concentrating. SOI's parabolic move reflects the market treating Photonics-SOI wafers as a structural bottleneck for CPO adoption. BESIY (BE Semiconductor) rode the same wave on die-attach and advanced packaging exposure. TSEM continues to benefit from specialty foundry demand, while AXTI held green on InP substrate scarcity narrative despite thin volume.
$IQE, $GFS: IQE gave back -2.65% as the OTC liquidity drag persists despite the same end-market tailwinds. GFS slipped -1.28%, still digesting the auto/industrial overhang even as its RF-SOI partnership with Soitec positions it well longer term.
2. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
$COHR, $MRVL: Coherent and Marvell both green, tracking the 800G/1.6T transceiver cycle. COHR +1.80% is steady rather than explosive here, which suggests the easy money in pluggable transceiver names may already be priced.
$LITE, $CIEN, $AAOI: The transceiver and networking names that should be benefiting from the same AI optical build are instead lagging. LITE -2.15% and CIEN -2.68% are giving back recent gains. AAOI -4.41% continues to trade heavy, potentially reflecting profit-taking after its tariff-exemption-driven run.
$GLW, $VIAV: Passive optical infrastructure names drifting lower. Not broken, just not where the heat is today.
3. Semiconductor Equipment, Test and Infrastructure
$AEHR, $FORM, $KEYS, $TER, $ATEYY: AEHR +3.84% is the standout, continuing its recent strength on wafer-level burn-in demand tied to SiC and compound semi production ramps. ATEYY (Advantest) surged +8.65%, a strong signal that test infrastructure spending for advanced packaging and photonics ICs is accelerating. FORM flat, KEYS and TER slightly red but neither signaling anything concerning.
4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
$LWLG, $ALMU, $POET, $SIVEF: LWLG bucked the early-stage selloff with a +7.20% move, potentially on renewed interest in EO polymer modulators as CPO architectures demand novel modulator solutions beyond conventional LiNbO3. ALMU -7.36% gave back gains on thin float dynamics. POET -8.01% is the direct casualty of the Wolfpack short report, which weaponized the PFIC tax angle right before the April 15 deadline. SIVEF -11.50% is the worst performer on the board, dragged down by contagion from the short seller thesis hitting the pre-revenue photonic integration cohort."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2043803655737147597"}}, "rest_id": "2044145584949674252", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "35494", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2044554804744360306", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/android" rel="nofollow">Twitter for Android</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "29679", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
我理解的SIVE
聊完AEHR 和 AAOI 还有很大的涨幅空间
来聊聊目前上涨空间最大的一个公司SIVE
今天SIVE就+30%

SIVE

这个公司basically就是做让AI芯片传输数据的激光器
AI芯片是需要光用silicon photonics技术来传输数据的

为什么要用光来传输数据呢？
当电信号的频率极高的时候 电子不再在整根铜线内部跑 而是在铜线的表面跑 会让电阻指数级别飙升
数据中心的两张显卡交换数据的时候速度会达到1.6T
这么快的速度电信号跑不到1-2米就会衰减成没有办法识别的代码
而且为了对抗铜线的衰减，只能加大电压去推信号，产生的热量很高不仅费电还危险
最重要最重要的一点是光没有质量+没有电荷
在玻璃里传输的时候没有电阻
总结一句话来说就是
铜线的带宽是有极限的 而光的带宽是无限的
这也是为什么我前几年最大持仓的CRDO有压力的原因

在实验室做一个好激光器不难 但是难在100毫米的inp晶圆上 想量产laser arrays(我都不知道这个中文叫什么 like 激光器矩阵？somebody teach me）并且质量很高 是非常难的 所以大公司都找sive定

现在他们确定的订单有50M预定订单有450M
2028 2029年
我预估300M营收 x 20ps 5-6B的市值 这还是很保守的
今天市值才660M
660M x 10-15？

聊聊为什么还有很高空间 排除公司本身
1. 这个公司并没有在美国nasdaq上市
而是在瑞典nasdaq上市 来这边上市估计股价更高
想在fidelity上买还要加50🔪
2. 市值太小 like 660M的市值我都没办法布置sizeable 仓位 更别说其他manage billions的fund manager
3. 还有个x因素 x上一个很火的大神 @aleabitoreddit  粉丝量很大 分析ai公司非常厉害
我也在关注 好像他成sive 1%+的大股东了

我的预测 sive会被大公司收购 可能瞬间10x
我预估Avgo或者Marvell吧 这两家最有可能收购他 来补自己的短板

我的public accounts 持仓更新完整在图2:
Sive没有放进去因为大部分朋友自己的broker买不到
BE +53%
Aehr+59%
aaoi+74%
mu+30%
lwlg+45%
ichr+20%
uct+ 24%
等等

Join me
我所有持仓都将公开

$sive

text:
GAN is the way forward … lets discuss some alpha in the Photonics Supercycle: Signals in AIXA earnings call

$AIXA had earnings call and popped up another +11%, bringing YTD upside to +130% and 1 yr upside to +288%.

You guys know that am already invested in Aixtron ($AIXA) and have written about this German champion in the past, framing it as a quintessential "hidden monopoly" within the semiconductor equipment space. I also think that this is early innings of a massive Photonics Supercycle. As AI clusters migrate from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T optical modules, the bottleneck for AI scaling has shifted from the H100/B200 compute engine to the interconnect fabric itself. You cannot run a frontier model if the data can’t move between the GPU and the memory stack fast enough. Aixtron sits at the very mouth of this funnel.

I would not regurgitate the numbers here, rather analyse and unpack the things I consider important with impact on overall AI and semisector. 

The Great Divergence: GaN vs. SiC

One of the most tactical takeaways from the call was the stark contrast between Gallium Nitride (GaN) and Silicon Carbide (SiC).

🐊 SiC Overcapacity: Management was candid about Silicon Carbide being in a state of "substantial market overcapacity," with SiC expected to be only 10% of their revenue this year. This is a warning shot for pure-play SiC substrate and device makers who are still digesting inventory.

🐊 The GaN Opportunity: Conversely, GaN is entering a golden era. Management expects AI data centers to become the largest single application for GaN power semiconductors. Because GaN is significantly more efficient than traditional silicon at high frequencies, it is becoming the standard for the power supply units (PSUs) that feed power-hungry AI servers.

For those looking to play the GaN trend beyond the equipment layer, keep an eye on these players:

• Navitas Semiconductor ($NVTS) – A US-based pure-play leader in GaN power ICs.

• Infineon Technologies ($IFX.DE / $IFNNY) – A European giant with a massive, growing footprint in GaN following their acquisition of GaN Systems.

• STMicroelectronics ($STM) – Another European heavyweight with strong GaN-on-Silicon capabilities.

• BluGlass ($BLG.AX) – An Australian-listed speculative play involved in specialized

🐊 Datacom Doubling: Management is forecasting that demand for datacom lasers will more than double YoY in 2026. This is a massive green flag for the laser makers themselves, such as Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR), who are racing to expand Indium Phosphide (InP) capacity. (am invested in both)

🐊The "Tool of Record" for AI Interconnects

The standout signal from the call was the performance of the G10-AsP system. Management confirmed it has become the "tool of record" for the next generation of photonic components

🐊 Order Intake Explosion: Order intake hit €171 million, a ~30% YoY increase that blew past analyst expectations. Crucially, 65% of these orders came from the optoelectronics segment

🐊Implications for the Memory (HBM) Ecosystem

While we often view memory through the lens of SK Hynix or Micron $MU, the Aixtron update provides a critical "read-through" for how these chips will be connected in the future.

🐊 Structural Bottlenecks: For those holding SK Hynix, $DRAM or Micron ($MU), the shift toward optical interconnects is the solution to the "power wall" that threatens to limit HBM performance. Aixtron’s order backlog suggests the equipment for this transition is being installed now.

🐊 Near-Stack Integration: The acceleration of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) means that optical engines will eventually sit directly on the package alongside High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks.

Look the "Photonics Supercycle" isn't a theory anymore it’s appearing in the capex cycles of the world's largest chipmakers.

And remember, none of this is financial advice. The crocodile can make errors. So do your own research.

"text": "La prochaine microcaps Française qui va péter serait Memscap ? >La boîte a 39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "340303", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045643800786395522", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "206", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
✏️LESSON EXTRACTION FROM SUCCESSFUL PITCH

Soitec ( $SOI.PA) by @jasons_chips 

121% gain since the March 10 pitch.

📋 WHAT THE PITCH ARGUED:
🔹 Mobile segment was at a cyclical floor with inventory clearing.
🔹 "Smart Cut" technology created a near-monopoly in photonics substrates.
🔹 Management was deliberately low-balling future growth projections.

✅ WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED:
🔹 Q3 sequential revenue beat confirmed the mobile trough was in.
🔹 1.6T networking demand pulled photonics orders forward significantly.

🎯 THE SURPRISE:
The market re-rated the stock as a pure AI infrastructure play almost overnight. It shifted from a "boring" materials cyclical to a critical networking bottleneck provider.

MY TAKE: We expected a slow recovery as inventory cleared. Instead, NVIDIA's commentary on Co-Packaged Optics triggered a massive institutional rotation into the name. This shows how quickly a value setup turns into a momentum play when the macro tailwind arrives.
But if I am honest, this performance is likely a result of important people mentioning this company on X 😀

text:
The Copper Wall Account 🧱➡️💡

My thesis: The next wave of AI infrastructure is the shift from copper interconnects to silicon photonics and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in data centers. Power efficiency and bandwidth demands are forcing the change and I’m adding exposure to quantum computing.

Here’s my initial DCA plan:

Starting tomorrow: $5/week into each of these 10 stocks 

$TSM $AVGO $MRVL $LITE $AAOI $CRDO $FN $TSEM $IONQ $HON

•  Photonics/AI infrastructure play: TSM, AVGO, MRVL, LITE, AAOI, CRDO, FN, TSEM

•  Quantum long-shot: IONQ + HON (via Quantinuum)

I’ll post weekly updates on performance, news, and any adjustments.

If the AI buildout accelerates and silicon photonics/CPO gets widely adopted, I expect strong returns by 2030.

If IONQ or Quantinuum leads in quantum, this 10-year DCA could compound beautifully by 2040.

Who’s in on the next tech wave? Drop your thoughts 👇

text:
This is the most comprehensive photonics stack there is. 

L1: Materials & Substrates

The foundation. Without this, nothing above it exists.
L1 companies grow the raw crystal wafers – InP, GaAs, Ge that silicon can’t replace for high-speed photonics.

Key names: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE, $AIXA, $COHR, $LASR

Top performer: AXT (AXTI) +6,837%

InP demand exploded for AI datacenter optical connectivity. Q3 2025 InP revenues up 250%+ sequentially. Doubling capacity by end of 2026. 

L2: Active Photonic Devices

This is where light gets made.
Lasers, modulators, photodetectors. In a CPO world, these get embedded directly into switch ASICs – no longer pluggable add-ons. That’s a structural demand shift, not a cycle.

Key names: $LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SIVEF, $AAOI, $MTSI, $SMTC

Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685%

Named NVIDIA collaborator on Spectrum-X silicon photonics. Recently received the largest single CPO laser purchase commitment in company history.

L3: Electro-Optical Connectivity ICs

The translators. Silicon that bridges optical and electrical worlds.

TIAs, drivers, DSPs, SerDes, retimers. As speeds push to 800G and 1.6T, this layer becomes a chokepoint.

Key names: $MTSI, $SMTC, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $MXLV

Top performer: Credo (CRDO) +361%

Not a pure photonics play – but every 800G and 1.6T deployment runs through their AEC and SerDes IP.

L4: PIC Platform & IP

The brain of the photonic system.
Waveguides, modulators, and photodetectors on a single chip. Highest-IP layer in the stack. Whoever locks platform relationships here wins the next decade.

Key names: $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO, $IOLI, $EOPT (Ayar Labs, Lightmatte)

Top performer: Innolight +948%

Partnered with Tower Semi on a breakthrough SiPh platform that cuts lasers per module in half.

L5: Foundry & Process Platform

You need a fab. Not just any fab.
SiPh fabrication needs specialized processes – TFLN, SiPh COUPE, SoIC – that only a handful of foundries can run globally.

Key names: $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $TSM

Top performer: Tower Semi (TSEM) +542%

SiPh revenue hit ~$52M in Q3 2025 alone, up 70% YoY. Full-year 2025 target: $220M+. Q4 annualized run rate: $320M+. Investing $650M to triple SiPh shipments by mid-2026.

L6A: Engine & Module

Where the optical engine becomes a product – 400G, 800G, 1.6T modules.
Most commoditized layer today. CPO transition will redraw it entirely.

Key names: $COHR, $LITE, $AAOI, $FN, $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO

Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685%

Vertically integrated across L2 and L6A. Supply locked by hyperscalers. Pricing power persists.

L6B: Infrastructure

Fiber and cable. The physical nervous system of every AI cluster.
Boring until hyperscalers start spending $50B+ on datacenter campuses. Then it’s anything but.

Key names: $GLW, $OTCPK:SMOKY, $PRY, $APH, $FJKUF

Top performer: Fujikura (5803.TSE) +748%

Japanese fiber giant. Direct beneficiary of AI intra-datacenter cabling demand going parabolic.

L6C: Transport

Moves data between datacenters across metro and long-haul fiber.
As AI clusters get larger and more distributed, inter-datacenter bandwidth demand compounds. This layer doesn’t slow down.

Key names: $CIEN, $NOK, $CSCO

Top performer: Ciena (CIEN) +777%

WaveLogic coherent optics is the dominant long-haul platform in North America and Europe

L7: Test & Qualification

The final gatekeeper. Every photonic device passes through here before shipping.

Burn-in, optical test, IC test, wafer test. Often overlooked. Often the highest-margin layer.

Key names: $KEYS, $TER, $ATE, $FRT, $AEHR, $VIAV, $COHU

Top performer: Aehr Test (AEHR) +954%

Shipped the world’s first production wafer-level burn-in system for AI processors. Now diversified into silicon photonics, GaN, and hard disk drives.

Great job on putting it together  @damnang2

Key names: $LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SIVEF, $AAOI, $MTSI, $SMTC Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Named NVIDIA collaborator on Spectrum-X silicon photonics. Recently received the largest single CPO laser purchase commitment in company history. L3: Electro-Optical Connectivity ICs The translators. Silicon that bridges optical and electrical worlds. TIAs, drivers, DSPs, SerDes, retimers. As speeds push to 800G and 1.6T, this layer becomes a chokepoint. Key names: $MTSI, $SMTC, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $MXLV Top performer: Credo (CRDO) +361% Not a pure photonics play – but every 800G and 1.6T deployment runs through their AEC and SerDes IP. L4: PIC Platform & IP The brain of the photonic system. Waveguides, modulators, and photodetectors on a single chip. Highest-IP layer in the stack. Whoever locks platform relationships here wins the next decade. Key names: $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO, $IOLI, $EOPT (Ayar Labs, Lightmatte) Top performer: Innolight +948% Partnered with Tower Semi on a breakthrough SiPh platform that cuts lasers per module in half. L5: Foundry & Process Platform You need a fab. Not just any fab. SiPh fabrication needs specialized processes – TFLN, SiPh COUPE, SoIC – that only a handful of foundries can run globally. Key names: $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $TSM Top performer: Tower Semi (TSEM) +542% SiPh revenue hit ~$52M in Q3 2025 alone, up 70% YoY. Full-year 2025 target: $220M+. Q4 annualized run rate: $320M+. Investing $650M to triple SiPh shipments by mid-2026. L6A: Engine & Module Where the optical engine becomes a product – 400G, 800G, 1.6T modules. Most commoditized layer today. CPO transition will redraw it entirely. Key names: $COHR, $LITE, $AAOI, $FN, $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Vertically integrated across L2 and L6A. Supply locked by hyperscalers. Pricing power persists. L6B: Infrastructure Fiber and cable. The physical nervous system of every AI cluster. Boring until hyperscalers start spending $50B+ on datacenter campuses. Then it’s anything but. Key names: $GLW, $OTCPK:SMOKY, $PRY, $APH, $FJKUF Top performer: Fujikura (5803.TSE) +748% Japanese fiber giant. Direct beneficiary of AI intra-datacenter cabling demand going parabolic. L6C: Transport Moves data between datacenters across metro and long-haul fiber. As AI clusters get larger and more distributed, inter-datacenter bandwidth demand compounds. This layer doesn’t slow down. Key names: $CIEN, $NOK, $CSCO Top performer: Ciena (CIEN) +777% WaveLogic coherent optics is the dominant long-haul platform in North America and Europe L7: Test & Qualification The final gatekeeper. Every photonic device passes through here before shipping. Burn-in, optical test, IC test, wafer test. Often overlooked. Often the highest-margin layer. Key names: $KEYS, $TER, $ATE, $FRT, $AEHR, $VIAV, $COHU Top performer: Aehr Test (AEHR) +954% Shipped the world’s first production wafer-level burn-in system for AI processors. Now diversified into silicon photonics, GaN, and hard disk drives. text: @ParadisLabs @ParadisLabs I've actually been doing quite a bit of research in the overall photonics sector (outside of Data Center/AI). $JEN came up in my research, I can send over my write-up if you would like. Check DMs.

@PhotonicsTrackr @PepInvestStocks Yeah $LASE doesn’t really fit the layer taxonomy - they’re an end-application laser play (industrial cutting/welding/marking) rather than a supplier into the photonics stack. Interesting angle is the pivot into defense/directed energy.

@mattpurdy89 @PepInvestStocks $LASE is interesting- we didn’t include it in our layer mapping. It does call itself a photonics-based industrial products company but we couldn’t find any evidence of it fitting into our layer taxonomy. 

Curious about your thoughts. https://t.co/JHPdDb4P43

text:
I'm confused how Jenoptik, $JEN, is up 65% YTD?

The "Photonics" label is doing a lot of heavy lifting for some pretty shaky fundamentals:

-> $JEN is a Tier 2 supplier, but getting priced like a monopoly. High capex + R&D exposure without pricing power of true lithography giants like $ASML

-> ​€100M capex on their new fab is just going to kill margins. With the semi-recovery looking lumpy in Q2 2026, the fixed cost drag could lead to a huge earnings miss by end of yr.
​
-> ​26x P/E for a business that is still 30% low-margin traffic safety and automotive metrology. The "pure photonics" rebrand is a marketing ploy, and not a financial or operational reality yet.

-> In the Photonics segment, Chinese competitors are eroding the moat in micro-optics and laser modules. $JEN is being squeezed between the high-end (Zeiss) and the low-cost (MLOptic).

$JEN is just a cyclical hardware firm w/ significant non-core baggage being priced like a secular AI winner.

embedded_text:
Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.

text:
Asian supply chain leaks just revealed the Holy Grail of wearables, and the market is completely missing the underlying silicon photonics chokepoint. 🚨 

Let’s connect the dots on Point 6 regarding the Apple Watch Ultra and the non-invasive blood glucose sensor. If you want to know who is building the light engine, look at the attached slide from $SIVE / $SIVEF. 🧵👇

1️⃣ The Leak: Miniaturized Lasers for Health
According to the supply chain leak below, Apple is targeting April next year for its blood glucose feature. The tech? Non-invasive spectroscopy using miniaturized lasers to collect reflected wavelengths through the skin.

2️⃣ The F100 Client Connection
Look at the attached slide from Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE). A mystery "US Fortune 100" client has invested ~$18M in NRE to develop custom Indium Phosphide (InP) optical sensors for "Consumer devices" (notice the smartwatch icon). Apple is Top 5 on the Fortune 100.

3️⃣ The Timeline Matches Perfectly
Sivers delivered an order of 50,000 chips in Q1 2024 to this F100 client. 50k is not mass production—it is the exact volume required for EVT/DVT (Engineering & Design Validation Testing) prototype runs. A prototype run in early 2024 aligns perfectly with an aggressive commercial launch in Spring 2027.

4️⃣ The True TAM (Total Addressable Market)
The slide explicitly states that when this project enters the volume phase, the Request For Proposal (RFP) ranges from $30M to several $100M per year.

While retail investors are looking at historical P/E ratios of a legacy telecom business, they are completely blind to the fact that $SIVE has spent years building the miniaturized InP laser engine for the biggest consumer health revolution of the decade.
The data is right there for anyone willing to do the math. 1+1=2. 🍏⌚️

⚠️ Disclaimer: Supply chain leaks are unverified rumors by nature and timelines can shift. However, investing is about connecting high-probability data points. The technological and chronological overlap here is simply too precise to ignore. As always, DYOR.

text:
I’m now long $POET

$POET is one of the wildest turnaround stories in photonics, not so long ago I was bearish with the hard dilution around this company. 

Rewind to early 2024. Company was near insolvent. Nearly ran out of cash. Forced into an emergency raise. Share count has nearly 4x’d in two years.
Fast forward to today:
+$ 313M cash on the balance sheet (vs ~$1.3B market cap)
+ Just raised $150M in January at $7.25 from institutional investors
+ 152M shares out vs 40M-ish pre-2024
+ Marvell orders now confirmed by the CFO
+ Foxconn and Luxshare partnerships already disclosed
+ Strategic tie-up with $SIVE on integrated light sources

The dilution was brutal. But look at what it bought: a wafer-level optical interposer platform that Marvell, Foxconn, Luxshare, and Sivers are all plugged into.

The old POET was a story stock with $41K in revenue. The new POET just 25x’d revenue YoY and is embedded in the CPO supply chain right next to $SIVE

A raising phoenix as it seems.

I’m long since yesterday. Great continuation today up 20% and still a long way to re rate.

Ooo the irony...You point this out and you immediately get two Swedish jante guys chasing you...

Since the spending time on the wrong things and don't see the photonics ecosystem revaluation..they don't get it..

This picture show a "peer" and customer to Sivers..Just look at the valuation..Sivers looks cheep in this comparison. What the don't get is this revaluation is about something else, not at all about SIVE in it self..it is about the ecosystem below AI, hyperscalers, Nvidia/ AMD and so on...I told them all many years
 ago that this will happen. But the same thing then but but EBIT is bad...EBIT is bad still due to write off capitalized R&D and M&A...mostly none CF items. 
God speed! 🍺

text: Fiber laser with anti-collision system made in Poland. Kimla builds their fiber lasers using US/German supplier IPG Photonics for the source. Most of the machine is from components made in Poland. It's actually clean, it doesn't fund the Chinese.
embedded_text:
Building a fast fiber laser is easy
protecting it from collisions at  speed is the actual problem

Kimla Anti collision system reacts 20X faster than the competition 

linear motors + height sensors that doesnt lag speed 
without safety is just expensive downtime simply waiting https://t.co/e6MNiVB5wO

The $2.3B Infinera acquisition has created a vertically integrated optical/photonics powerhouse addressing AI data center bottlenecks with high-speed coherent pluggables (1.6T+), lower power, and scalable DCI fabrics. 

OFC 2026 demos and strong hyperscaler orders (+17% optical growth) signal acceleration in the 11–18% CAGR interconnect market.

Combined with NVIDIA’s $1B investment and AI-RAN trials (T-Mobile, SoftBank), Nokia builds end-to-end AI-native networks from wireless edge (RAN leadership + 6G foundations) to optical core—few peers match this stack.

I’m seeing some pushback on an equipment level, and I want to explain a little bit on that. @vlmkapital wrote a good post on how he views the investment case from the equipement level (link in comments) and I want to expand on things there are missing. Heads up.. this one is longer than usual. Most of the pushback I’m seeing X on sectors is superficial, and the argument I’m making here has multiple layers that all depend on each other. So if you want the short version, you won’t find it here💁‍♂️ @GyujinAAIG is working on the full scope for AAIG, and will publish the research soon but I will lay out the strategy part from my side already a bit. The lens that is being used when tracking companies like LPKF and the timelines is a technology adoption kind of lens. Will give @FinnStockinger also a tag in this part. But they’re missing the capital allocation and incentive lens, which is freaking crucial for this thesis. I’m going to explain a little bit why, and later I’m also going to use Aehr Test Systems as some kind of example of what happened there. So what’s the situation on the ground now? Semco expects the glass substrate era to enter into full swing around 2027-ish. They are currently developing samples with customers, and Absolics is also targeting mass production in 2027 and moving ahead with preparation. They got SKC approval for a capital increase of around $690 million, allocating roughly $410 million into Absolics for product development. So there are real commitments there, no doubt. Also, I’m not saying to totally ignore the glass thesis. We are watching it closely as well. I’m just pointing out some crucial things. If you look carefully at what is actually being built, Absolics is building a facility of around 12,000 square meters and is in some pre-qualification discussions with AMD and AWS. That’s not HVM. That is a very expensive demonstration phase, which is totally normal. It happens a lot. Companies build pilot lines, run sample production, and do customer qualification runs. It’s an investment to stay in the technology race, but it’s not revenue-generating HVM deployment. Keep that in mind. The reality is that it’s a qualification facility running at a small fraction of the scale needed to move the industry and the economics. I will elaborate further on that a bit later. Semco and Absolics are substrate manufacturers, and they’re building capacity for a market whose architectural gateway has not yet opened. It actually has been delayed. And this is where people keep getting confused — substrate manufacturer ramp-up plans are not the same thing as equipment-supplier inflection. SEMCO and Absolics don’t decide whether glass enters the HVM stack at scale. They’re positioning themselves for it. "text": "La prochaine microcaps Française qui va péter serait Memscap ? >La boîte a 39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "340306", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045589392387920373", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "7970", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Remember, I lived next door to a confirmed robotics and photonics expert, Mr. Pierrick Vulliez. This should not be difficult to understand—even for Grok.

In the footage, the hand movements of the man standing in the trench align point-for-point with the robot digger’s shovel movements. This is one of several videos I’ve posted over the past few days suggesting how closely a human operator must be controlling the machine, and all involve precise hand or head movements by the controller from what I’ve seen.

If the trench activity were human controlling another human that would imply a level of coordination I’m not prepared to interpret beyond what my current evidence supports.

text:
$LASE — Valye Company Analysis
Laser Photonics Corporation’s latest quarterly filing reveals expanding top-line efforts driven by strategic acquisitions, notably in pharmaceutical laser applications through Control Micro Systems. Despite substantial net losses and liquid
Key points:
• Q4 2025 results show widened net losses but progressing revenue from expanded product portfolio.
• Acquisition of Control Micro Systems broadens addressable market into recession-resistant pharmaceutical manufacturing.
• Vertically integrated production supports cost control, protects proprietary technology, and enhances pricing flexibility.
• Laser blasting disrupts traditional abrasive methods across automotive, aerospace, healthcare, shipbuilding, and nuclear sectors.
• Government contracts include innovative defense projects like the Laser Shield Anti-Drone System (LSAD).
Read: https://t.co/gwKtiCP7um

Especially if you miss on the opportunity - it is easier to justify with "WSB".

text:
On $CRDO's acquisition of Dust Photonics.

The $TSEM foundry thesis is gaining traction, and it makes sense given Tower's SiPh capabilities.

Worth adding: $ALAB did the same thing last October, acquiring aiXscale Photonics. A copper-first interconnect company buying a small photonics startup to get into optics. Both companies made the same call.

I haven't seen anyone put these two side by side yet.

https://t.co/6ZZhAm3XsA
embedded_text:
Excellent episode. Don't feel bad if you didn't know about Dust Photonics pre-deal. They said $TSEM was probably the fab for Dust, so if $CRDO blows out the product, it is incremental demand for TSEM. Also, an interesting liquid cooling angle.

As it comes to CPU, my @openclaw

As it comes to CPU, my @openclaw built and deployed a local embeddings model and vector search on our @digitalocean VPS last night. All of this requires incremental CPU for my cloud, probably less compute-efficient than doing it through OpenAI.

text:
Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.
New post to educate about SATCOM ⤵️

text:
源杰科技（https://t.co/ZBjZH3oFsS）是激光芯片IDM厂商，2013年1月由张欣刚（ZHANG XINGANG，美国籍，1970年生）在陕西咸阳创立。

他清华本科、南加州大学材料科学背景，曾任Luminent研发经理、Source Photonics研发总监。现任董事长兼总经理。

妹妹张欣颖任董事（持股约3.43%）。核心技术副总潘彦廷等。

主营2.5G-50G磷化铟激光芯片，用于数据中心、5G、FTTH等。2022年科创板上市，近期AI算力需求推高股价超茅台成A股新股王。官网：https://t.co/ekEJYAQGbk

The photonics move has been sharp. A lot of names already up big.

Still watching $LITE, $AAOI and $SMTC for cleaner entries. Rather buy consolidation than chase strength.
The AI networking theme isn't done. Just waiting for the right setup.

Anyone else still in this space? https://t.co/CrtRZYLS0I

Laser Photonics Corp received a notice on April 20, 2026, from Nasdaq stating it failed to comply with listing rules due to not filing its Form 10-K for 2025, but subsequently filed the form, resolving the issue by April 21, 2026. The matter is now closed and the company has regained compliance with Nasdaq regulations.

Copper Wall thesis is sharp—the shift to silicon photonics/CPO + quantum is real for AI power efficiency.

$5 each into the 12 stocks weekly = $60/wk, ~$15,600 over 5 years.

Silicon photonics market is forecast to grow 25-30% CAGR to ~$9-11B by 2030 (multiple sources). Quantum adds 20-40% upside potential.

In a strong AI adoption scenario, this basket (heavy on semis/photonics leaders like TSM/AVGO/MRVL + speculative IONQ) could compound 20-35% annualized on average—turning it into $28k-$45k by 2031 via DCA.

High volatility/risk though—some names are small-cap bets. Solid long-term play if the buildout accelerates. I'll watch updates. 🚀

This was combined from raw information discovery of the decade that $MSFT Quantum was their buyer. text: Insight into behind the scenes of $CRDO acquisition Dust Photonics from the it’s PE owner. 👀 truly seems pivotal
embedded_text:

Great team, great company.   Dust designs photonic ICs (PICs) and engines that are foundational to Silicon Photonics. Silicon Photonic pluggables integrate multiple optical functions – traditionally", "id_str": "2044410644301046031", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 17, "reply_count": 26, "retweet_count": 34, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "340387261"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "1393596223433711616", "indices": [26, 39], "name": "Atreides Management, LP", "screen_name": "atreidesmgmt"}]}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNDQ0MTA2NDM5NDQ1NDIyMDg=", "text": "Nice to see Credo acquire @atreidesmgmt portfolio company DustPhotonics. Great team, great company.   Dust designs photonic ICs (PICs) and engines that are foundational to Silicon Photonics. Silicon Photonic pluggables integrate multiple optical functions – traditionally implemented with discrete components – into a single component called a PIC. This saves space and power while improving scalability.     Perhaps most importantly, PICs can feed multiple optical lanes with a smaller number of lasers, relative to traditional approaches typically requiring a discrete laser for each lane (e.g., 8x200G lasers for a 1.6T module). Timely given we are heading into a severe shortage of lasers. Please note that there will still be a laser shortage even if Dust’s PICs are broadly adopted.   Thermal and power-density limitations for pluggables will also eventually make adding more and more lasers impractical.  Silicon Photonics and PICs essentially create another axis for scaling bandwidth. And obviously, long-term, Silicon Photonics is essential for CPO.   PICs feel similar to RF in the early days of cellular - quite a bit of black magic. There are only a few companies successfully supplying datacom PICs today at scale – Dust is one of them.     Ronnen and team have been excellent operators and partners, and their products are well respected across the datacom ecosystem. Over $500m in optical revenue for Credo is material and I do think HyperLume was a smart bet for them - starting to hear more positive feedback about MicroLEDs from our venture portcos and the hyperscalers. Copper, CPO, Pluggables, MicroLEDs should all win for the foreseeable future in different applications in different hyperscaler networking topologies: networking is taking huge share of the datacenter BoM everywhere.   Coherent training clusters are increasing in size to enable ever larger models. A larger coherent cluster is much more networking intensive. And then the larger models trained on these larger clusters require larger switched scale-up domains to inference economically, which is again more networking intensive. Rubin and Trainium 4 will have much larger switched scale-up domains and we may need these systems deployed at scale to enable the broad availability of 10 trillion plus parameter models like Mythos. Networking, especially switched scale-up networking, should be the fastest growing part of the datacenter for the next several years.   Switched scale-up networking (almost all copper with some optical beginning late next year) > scale across (optical obviously) > scale-out (first place for CPO) from a growth perspective next 3 years imo. We will be using copper well into the 2030s and somewhat ironic that the growth of optical is likely to drive accelerated growth for copper in the near term relative to the strange zero-sum thinking I occasionally see here and in some sell-side notes."}}}, "rest_id": "2044410644301046031", "source": "Twitter for iPad", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "122018", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2044492573645869108", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "326", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The 3-year rule holds for commoditizable bottlenecks: DRAM, NAND, legacy logic. It breaks for capital-intensive or IP-protected ones: ASML EUV is 9 years and counting, TSMC leading-edge over a decade, Soitec/Shin-Etsu on SOI wafers 15+ years. Photonics supply chain fits the second bucket, not the first.

On 'meme-like' valuations, the right benchmark isn't Street targets, it's what the market already pays for rerated peers. Coherent trades at ~8.4x EV/Sales, growing 22% Y/Y, with Nvidia owning $2B of equity. That's the anchor for a fully-discovered photonics name this cycle.

Checking the supply chain against it: Soitec ~4x EV/Sales (50% discount to COHR), IQE ~6x (in-play takeover), LPKF ~2x. Those are meaningful discounts to the rerated comp, not premiums.

Where you're right: names that have run 500%+ in months on sub-$50M revenue can't be defended on peer-relative grounds either. Those are real outliers worth separating from the broader supply-chain story.

Duration holds, most of the supply chain trades at discounts to the rerated peer, some individual names are genuinely overextended. Bottleneck durability and single-name multiples aren't the same question.

text:
Most finance Twitter talks about semiconductors.

Almost nobody on finance Twitter has actually built one.

Trade Whisperer has 21 years in HBM, DRAM, and NAND plus graduate research in silicon photonics and lasers.

His AMA is open right now.

If you have any real exposure to $MU, $SNDK, $TSM, or the broader semi complex, this is a rare window.

Here's why it matters.

The memory thesis driving Micron's projected out-earning of Amazon and Meta by 2027 sits on one question.

Can Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron ramp HBM supply fast enough to capture current AI demand without triggering the classic memory glut cycle?

Analysts have views. Sell-side has models. Bulls and bears argue on podcasts.

Almost none of them have ever been inside a fab.

Trade Whisperer answers questions like "how long does HBM qualification actually take" and "why can't China catch up" from the engineering floor rather than the spreadsheet.

From the thread already running:

China catching up to HBM is "extremely hard. It's a moving target. If they ever figure out HBM3, others will be producing HBM4E by then."

Fab expansions "take decades for 100% utilization." The supply glut fear is priced for a cycle that can't physically happen as fast as the market assumes.

He's bullish on SK Hynix over Samsung specifically because of HBM3E leadership. That's not a trading view. That's an engineer's view on who actually makes the best chip right now.

These are the kind of reads that generate edge in semi positions.

Not because he's predicting prices. Because he's explaining physical constraints that determine what the prices eventually have to reflect.

If you hold MU or SNDK or any semi name, spend 20 minutes reading his replies.

Ask him something specific. Depth of qualification cycles. HBM4 timeline. Silicon photonics for interconnect. How packaging capacity actually constrains supply.

Finance Twitter is full of people who read earnings transcripts.

A semi engineer with 21 years of HBM experience doing an AMA is a different tier of information.

Use it while it's open.

Follow for more signals on when someone who actually knows something is worth listening to in public.

text:
Totally agree, photonics looks set to shine bright in the 2030s! 🚀
I love how it can be made using the same manufacturing lines as regular computer chips. That makes scaling up much easier. And combining it with terahertz tech on silicon for computing, communication, and even imaging all in one? Sounds like a powerful way forward.  Any interesting recent papers on this terahertz + photonics combo you’d recommend? Always fun chatting with someone who sees the potential too! ✨

text: @JackXJong Fair question. GlobalFoundries handles their wireless products, while WIN Semiconductors is used for high-volume photonics production, such as DFB lasers and arrays. The Glasgow fab is mainly focused on low-volume manufacturing, pilot runs, and continued technology development.

It is the company’s 100mm InP fab in Glasgow 🏭 Sivers owns and operates one of the few independent III-V photonic foundries with both advanced development capability and meaningful production capacity. This is not just an R&D line, but a qualified InP100 manufacturing platform with end-to-end capabilities — from epitaxy design and wafer processing to automated testing and qualification. The platform includes:  • Up to 5,000 wafer starts per year  • Automated test capacity of more than 2 million lasers per month  • ~$17M capex to expand toward ~$150M annual revenue capacity What makes this especially interesting is the etched-facet process with on-wafer optical coatings and testing. Rather than relying on slower, less efficient post-cleave workflows, Sivers can process, coat, and test on the full wafer before dicing — improving yield, cost, cycle time, and precision. Strategically, that matters. The in-house fab gives Sivers control over development, prototyping, qualification, and early-to-mid volume manufacturing, while partners like WIN Semiconductors provide a path to ultra-high-volume scale when needed. That combination supports the company’s shift from 1-to-1 to one-to-many: design once, qualify once, and sell repeatedly with known specs, shorter lead times, and better operating leverage. In an industry where many photonics players are fabless, owning this specialized manufacturing capability is a quiet but powerful advantage. Underrated foundation for $SIVE. text:
$AAOI is good for photonics and $CRDO for connectivity. Also $CLS and $COHR which does it all, and $AEHR for testing every semiconductor.

Software it's clearly $PLTR and $APP upside is massive as well as $NOW.

Defense - Drones are probably a better bet. I like $SWMR after the IPO.. price is more attractive with Erik Prince leading it. His connections and experience is bar none the best. Swarmer software runs on a drone as well increasing the TAM 100 fold.

Health - I was against this initially but the rise of peptides is real, so $HIMS and $IBRX for cancer in biotech.

Space - $PL (Google backed) and $RKLB is great. $ASTS is gonna have too much competition for me.

Robotics.. $TSLA - People need to start making that connection and just go long.

Insurance - I couldn't care less about. All scams. Probably United Healthcare though.

Cybersecurity is gonna be $CRWD - they're onboard for Anthropics company leadership board for security concerns around AI guardrails.

Quantum... still early for me to go all in, but $IONQ $INFQ are solid bets.

text:
No, not really similar in core tech. 

VIGO Photonics makes high-sensitivity HgCdTe (MCT) uncooled IR detectors/sensors for MWIR/LWIR detection—used in missiles, gas sensing, defense, and space (e.g. Curiosity rover). 

$LPTH focuses on IR optics (chalcogenide BlackDiamond lenses) + complete thermal imaging cameras/systems. Complementary pieces of the IR chain, not the same detection tech. 

(From the infographic + company sites)

@Frenchie_ Photonics is the angle, but the real story is aerospace.
MEMSCAP's pressure sensors go into planes and rockets - defense spending is booming and that's the actual catalyst here.

text: Photonics are taking off because most are small caps. They are easily modulated by the sudden investments that come in. Now having said that, the need for optics is REAL (reserach Ultra Rubin). Whole suupply chain is trying to figure how important they will become in the next 5 years. You need lasers but you need special materials for lasers but you also need special Co-Packaged-Optics for these to work in tandem with Silicon Chips... now you need more Pure Fiber Optics glass... now you need Optics Foundry as well. So yeah, the whole chain is moving. It's hype/real depending which company we are taking about. If psychology is what you're seeking stay invested on easy to understand optics players. I am staying with $LITE $COHR $GLW. $TSM $ASX for CPO.

Looking to elevate your laser system performance? The Crystech RTP Q-Switch offers fast, reliable electro-optic modulation designed for demanding applications in photonics and laser engineering.
Built with RTP (Rubidium Titanyl Phosphate) crystal technology, this Q-switch delivers:
🔹 High damage threshold
🔹 Fast switching speeds
🔹 Excellent thermal stability
🔹 No piezoelectric ringing

---

👀 Watch:

---

This isn’t easy mode anymore.
It’s "be right or get wrecked."

Volatility Contraction Pattern Analysis: $IPGP
Overview of IPGP Volatility Contraction Pattern (VCP)
IPG Photonics operates within the dynamic optical components sector, supplying lasers, optics, and robotic automation equipment. These products are essential for constructing the hardware required for artificial intelligence centers.
Stock Performance and Technical Analysis
I have been monitoring this stock for some time. It has demonstrated relative strength during the recent market downturn, as evidenced by its ability to maintain its lows and consolidate within a Volatility Contraction Pattern (VCP), a strategy popularized by Mark Minervini.
Position Entry and Strategy
Today, I initiated a quarter-sized position as the stock broke above what appears to be a bull flag. My plan is to gradually increase this position to a full allocation over the next few days, provided the stock can move above the horizontal resistance area. It is important to note that earnings are scheduled in 18 days, which allows sufficient time for a potential rally ahead of the event. For risk management, I will set a stop on the current position if there is a decisive close below the 21-day exponential moving average (EMA), indicated by the blue line on the chart.
